signals.title: GBP/USD — Gilt Yield Shock + Geopolitical Risk
signals.text: UK 30-year gilts hit 5.78%, the highest since 1998, while 10-year yields trade at 5.09%. Despite the massive yield surge, GBP/USD remains under pressure at 1.3541 as Middle East tensions escalate following reports of Iranian long-range missile development. The 0bp carry spread (Fed 3.75% vs BoE 3.75%) provides no structural buffer against safe-haven USD demand. Market is pricing fiscal instability rather than rate-driven support.

signals.title: NZD/USD — COT vs OIS Divergence + RBNZ
signals.text: NZD/USD COT LF net short -16,833 (long% 23.0%) shows speculators heavily fading the RBNZ's hawkish bias. With the RBNZ signaling a hike and the next meeting on 27 May, the pair faces significant short-squeeze risk if the central bank delivers. NZD/USD at 0.5888 is currently supported by a 150bp carry deficit vs USD, but crowded spec shorts are vulnerable to a hawkish surprise.

signals.title: AUD/USD — Positioning + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7185 following the RBA hike to 4.35%. COT LF net long +48,299 (74.8% long) indicates institutional conviction, while retail sentiment is 86% short. This extreme divergence suggests retail is aggressively fading the trend; a continuation higher triggers retail capitulation. The 60bp AUD carry premium over the Fed supports the bullish institutional stance.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Carry vs Intervention Risk
signals.text: USD/JPY at 157.87 remains supported by the 300bp Fed-BoJ carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs BoJ 0.75%). While JPY COT LF net short -88,543 remains crowded, the pair is holding below the 160.00 psychological level where BoJ intervention risk historically intensifies. VIX at 17.4 (normal volatility) suggests carry remains the dominant regime driver over safe-haven flows for now.
